latest.note: Camino del Norte; 4.1% of 531,000 total Compostelas in 2025. The longest non-Francés Camino but less walked than the Primitivo — demanding, less serviced, and favoured by experienced pilgrims.

annualPilgrims.sourceNotes: All counts from Solvitur Ambulando which mirrors Oficina del Peregrino. The Norte has plateaued at ~21,000 pilgrims per year since 2022, a contrast with the dramatic growth seen on the Portugués Coastal over the same period. The 2015 UNESCO inscription produced a small bump but did not change the route's overall demographic profile. See docs/data-sources.md.

demographics.topNationalitiesNote: Per-route 2024 figures from Solvitur Ambulando. The Norte has a noticeably higher French representation (6.0%) than most Caminos because it is the natural coastal continuation from France and is walked by French pilgrims starting at the Bidasoa border. 'Others' (~15.2%) not individually listed.
demographics.genderNote: Male majority of 56.5% (vs 43.4% female) — similar to the Primitivo's mountain-difficult demographic. The Norte's length, terrain variety, and reputation for being physically demanding correlate with an older-male skew compared to the Frances or Portugués.

motivationNote: Per-route 2024 figures from Solvitur Ambulando. Motivation distribution is broadly similar to the other major Caminos, with slightly higher non-religious share than Frances or Portugués.

comparisonWithFrances.en: In 2025 the Norte accounted for 4.1% of all Compostelas (21,521 pilgrims). It is the third-longest Camino after the Vía de la Plata and the Camino Francés, but only the 6th-most-walked — reflecting its reputation as physically demanding, less serviced than the Francés or Portugués, and requiring more self-sufficiency. Compared to other Caminos, Norte pilgrims skew older, more male, more European, and have a higher proportion of French walkers because the Bidasoa border crossing is a natural continuation from the French Camino network.

difficulty.en: The Norte accumulates more total elevation gain than either the Frances or the Primitivo because of constant short climbs and descents across coastal headlands — there is no single dramatic pass, just 34 days of up-and-down. The Bay of Biscay has the most variable weather of any Camino region; Atlantic storms can arrive suddenly in any month. Albergue density is significantly lower than on the Frances, and some stages have only one or two pilgrim-specific beds. The Norte is generally not recommended as a first-time pilgrim's Camino.

sources.name: Solvitur Ambulando — Yearly Demographics API
sources.notes: Primary source for 2003-2025 per-route counts and demographics. See docs/data-sources.md.
